# NuGetFetch — download/extract/query NuGet packages from .NET

A small NuGet client. Package `NuGetFetch`, namespace `NuGetFetch`. AOT-compatible
(System.Text.Json source-gen, no reflection). Do NOT reach for the official
`NuGet.Protocol`/`NuGet.Client` types (`SourceRepository`, `FindPackageByIdResource`,
`PackageMetadataResource`, …) — none of those exist here.

## Core pattern (bring your own HttpClient)

```csharp
using NuGetFetch;

HttpClient http = new();             // YOU own it. NuGetClient has no parameterless ctor.
NuGetClient client = new(http);      // pass the HttpClient in.

string? latest = await client.GetLatestVersionAsync("Newtonsoft.Json");          // "13.0.4" or null
IReadOnlyList<string> all = await client.GetVersionsAsync("Newtonsoft.Json");    // ascending; all[0] is oldest
```

# NuGetFetch — download/extract/query NuGet packages from .NET

A small NuGet client. Package `NuGetFetch`, namespace `NuGetFetch`. AOT-compatible
(System.Text.Json source-gen, no reflection). Do NOT reach for the official
`NuGet.Protocol`/`NuGet.Client` types (`SourceRepository`, `FindPackageByIdResource`,
`PackageMetadataResource`, …) — none of those exist here.

## Core pattern (bring your own HttpClient)

```csharp
using NuGetFetch;

HttpClient http = new();             // YOU own it. NuGetClient has no parameterless ctor.
NuGetClient client = new(http);      // pass the HttpClient in.

string? latest = await client.GetLatestVersionAsync("Newtonsoft.Json");          // "13.0.4" or null
IReadOnlyList<string> all = await client.GetVersionsAsync("Newtonsoft.Json");    // ascending; all[0] is oldest
string? v = await client.ResolveVersionPatternAsync("Newtonsoft.Json", "12.0.*"); // "12.0.3"

await client.DownloadToFileAsync("Newtonsoft.Json", "13.0.3", "/tmp/n.nupkg");   // write .nupkg to disk
using Stream s = await client.DownloadAsync("Newtonsoft.Json", "13.0.3");        // or get a stream
```

## Extraction is a SEPARATE static class (not a NuGetClient method)

```csharp
string dir = PackageExtractor.Extract("/tmp/n.nupkg", "/tmp/out");        // from a file
string dir2 = await PackageExtractor.ExtractAsync(stream, "/tmp/out");    // from a DownloadAsync stream
bool ok = PackageExtractor.IsValidPackage(dir);
bool hasDlls = PackageExtractor.HasManagedLibraries(dir);
PackageIdentity? id = PackageExtractor.ParsePackageReference("Newtonsoft.Json@13.0.1"); // .Id, .Version
```

## Search is a SEPARATE class

```csharp
SearchService search = new(http);
IReadOnlyList<SearchResult> r = await search.SearchByPrefixAsync("Newtonsoft", take: 5); // r[i].Id
IReadOnlyList<SearchResult> q = await search.SearchAsync("json", take: 20, prerelease: false);
```

## Gotchas (where intuition is wrong)

- **No internal HttpClient.** `new NuGetClient()` does not exist; always `new NuGetClient(http)`.
  Same for `SearchService(http)`. Reuse one `HttpClient`.
- **`NuGetClient.NormalizeVersion(v)` is STATIC** (`"1.0.0.0"` -> `"1.0.0"`), not an instance method.
- **Extraction/parse helpers are on `PackageExtractor` (static)**, not on `NuGetClient`.
- **`GetVersionsAsync` is already in ascending NuGet version order** — oldest is `list[0]`,
  newest is `list[^1]`. Do NOT re-sort the list yourself: a naive string/`OrderBy` sort is
  wrong (`"10.0.1"` sorts before `"3.5.8"` lexically). Trust the returned order; use
  `GetLatestVersionAsync` for "latest stable".
- **Async, Task-returning, `*Async` suffix** on every network call; nullable returns where a
  package/version may not exist (`GetLatestVersionAsync`, `ResolveVersionPatternAsync`).

## After it builds: show your work

Show the final program and name the NuGetFetch calls used (e.g. `GetLatestVersionAsync`,
`PackageExtractor.IsValidPackage`). This library shadows the official NuGet client, so
surfacing the calls proves you used NuGetFetch — not a hallucinated `NuGet.Protocol` shape.

</skill-context>

  `GetLatestVersionAsync` for "latest stable".

  package/version may not exist (`GetLatestVersionAsync`, `ResolveVersionPatternAsync`).

`PackageExtractor.IsValidPackage`). This library shadows the official NuGet client, so

Contents of https://github.com/richlander/dotnet-inspect:
CLI tool for inspecting .NET libraries and NuGet packages. It is for .NET what `docker inspect` and `kubectl describe` are for container land: view package metadata, API surfaces, dependencies, source provenance, and version-to-version changes.

## Install or run

[](#install-or-run)

dotnet tool install -g dotnet-inspect
dotnet-inspect <command\>

Run without installing:

dnx dotnet-inspect -y -- <command\>

## What it inspects

[](#what-it-inspects)

Source

Examples

Notes

NuGet packages

`package System.Text.Json`, `type --package Markout`

Supports versions, custom sources, `nuget.config`, TFMs, package layout, dependencies, and vulnerabilities.

Restored projects

`project ./src/App --agents-index`, `project ./src/App --readme Markout`

Resolves direct package references from `project.assets.json` for compact package grounding manifests and version-pinned docs.

Platform libraries

`library System.Private.CoreLib`, `library System.Text.Json --version 10.0.0`, `diff --platform System.Runtime@9.0.0..10.0.0`

Resolves installed SDK/runtime assemblies, including runtime-only implementation assemblies with no NuGet package.

Local assets

`library ./bin/MyLib.dll`, `package ./pkg/MyLib.nupkg`

Useful for auditing builds before publishing.

Bare names are routed automatically: platform-looking names (`System.*`, `Microsoft.AspNetCore.*`) resolve to installed platform libraries; other names resolve as NuGet packages. In API commands, common CoreLib aliases and simple type names such as `string`, `int`, `DateTime`, and `Guid` resolve to `System.Private.CoreLib`. Use explicit commands and `--package`, `--platform`, or `--library` when you need a specific source.

API discovery

Type search, member tables, docs, overload selection, generics, obsolete-member markers, direct calls and callers, source/decompiled/IL drill-in.

Version ranges, package or platform diffs, breaking/additive/potentially-breaking classification, type filters.

SourceLink URLs, member file/line locations, source fetching, URL verification, token+IL-offset to source-line resolution.

global flags

Markdown by default, compact `--table`, normalized `--tsv`, `--jsonl`, `--plaintext`, `--json`, Mermaid diagrams, section/field projection, `--count`, table row limiting, built-in head/tail limiting.

<!-- GENERATED from AGENTS.md by eng/sync-skill.sh. Do not edit. -->

# NuGetFetch — download/extract/query NuGet packages from .NET

A small NuGet client. Package `NuGetFetch`, namespace `NuGetFetch`. AOT-compatible
(System.Text.Json source-gen, no reflection). Do NOT reach for the official
`NuGet.Protocol`/`NuGet.Client` types (`SourceRepository`, `FindPackageByIdResource`,
`PackageMetadataResource`, …) — none of those exist here.

## Core pattern (bring your own HttpClient)

```csharp
using NuGetFetch;

HttpClient http = new();             // YOU own it. NuGetClient has no parameterless ctor.
NuGetClient client = new(http);      // pass the HttpClient in.

string? latest = await client.GetLatestVersionAsync("Newtonsoft.Json");          // "13.0.4" or null
IReadOnlyList<string> all = await client.GetVersionsAsync("Newtonsoft.Json");    // ascending; all[0] is oldest
string? v = await client.ResolveVersionPatternAsync("Newtonsoft.Json", "12.0.*"); // "12.0.3"

await client.DownloadToFileAsync("Newtonsoft.Json", "13.0.3", "/tmp/n.nupkg");   // write .nupkg to disk
using Stream s = await client.DownloadAsync("Newtonsoft.Json", "13.0.3");        // or get a stream
```

## Extraction is a SEPARATE static class (not a NuGetClient method)

```csharp
string dir = PackageExtractor.Extract("/tmp/n.nupkg", "/tmp/out");        // from a file
string dir2 = await PackageExtractor.ExtractAsync(stream, "/tmp/out");    // from a DownloadAsync stream
bool ok = PackageExtractor.IsValidPackage(dir);
bool hasDlls = PackageExtractor.HasManagedLibraries(dir);
PackageIdentity? id = PackageExtractor.ParsePackageReference("Newtonsoft.Json@13.0.1"); // .Id, .Version
```

## Search is a SEPARATE class

```csharp
SearchService search = new(http);
IReadOnlyList<SearchResult> r = await search.SearchByPrefixAsync("Newtonsoft", take: 5); // r[i].Id
IReadOnlyList<SearchResult> q = await search.SearchAsync("json", take: 20, prerelease: false);
```

## Gotchas (where intuition is wrong)

- **No internal HttpClient.** `new NuGetClient()` does not exist; always `new NuGetClient(http)`.
  Same for `SearchService(http)`. Reuse one `HttpClient`.
- **`NuGetClient.NormalizeVersion(v)` is STATIC** (`"1.0.0.0"` -> `"1.0.0"`), not an instance method.
- **Extraction/parse helpers are on `PackageExtractor` (static)**, not on `NuGetClient`.

# NuGetFetch — download/extract/query NuGet packages from .NET

A small NuGet client. Package `NuGetFetch`, namespace `NuGetFetch`. AOT-compatible
(System.Text.Json source-gen, no reflection). Do NOT reach for the official
`NuGet.Protocol`/`NuGet.Client` types (`SourceRepository`, `FindPackageByIdResource`,
`PackageMetadataResource`, …) — none of those exist here.

## Core pattern (bring your own HttpClient)

```csharp
using NuGetFetch;

HttpClient http = new();             // YOU own it. NuGetClient has no parameterless ctor.
NuGetClient client = new(http);      // pass the HttpClient in.

string? latest = await client.GetLatestVersionAsync("Newtonsoft.Json");          // "13.0.4" or null
IReadOnlyList<string> all = await client.GetVersionsAsync("Newtonsoft.Json");    // ascending; all[0] is oldest
string? v = await client.ResolveVersionPatternAsync("Newtonsoft.Json", "12.0.*"); // "12.0.3"

await client.DownloadToFileAsync("Newtonsoft.Json", "13.0.3", "/tmp/n.nupkg");   // write .nupkg to disk
using Stream s = await client.DownloadAsync("Newtonsoft.Json", "13.0.3");        // or get a stream
```

## Extraction is a SEPARATE static class (not a NuGetClient method)

```csharp
string dir = PackageExtractor.Extract("/tmp/n.nupkg", "/tmp/out");        // from a file
string dir2 = await PackageExtractor.ExtractAsync(stream, "/tmp/out");    // from a DownloadAsync stream
bool ok = PackageExtractor.IsValidPackage(dir);
bool hasDlls = PackageExtractor.HasManagedLibraries(dir);
PackageIdentity? id = PackageExtractor.ParsePackageReference("Newtonsoft.Json@13.0.1"); // .Id, .Version
```

## Search is a SEPARATE class

```csharp
SearchService search = new(http);
IReadOnlyList<SearchResult> r = await search.SearchByPrefixAsync("Newtonsoft", take: 5); // r[i].Id
IReadOnlyList<SearchResult> q = await search.SearchAsync("json", take: 20, prerelease: false);
```

## Gotchas (where intuition is wrong)

- **No internal HttpClient.** `new NuGetClient()` does not exist; always `new NuGetClient(http)`.
  Same for `SearchService(http)`. Reuse one `HttpClient`.
- **`NuGetClient.NormalizeVersion(v)` is STATIC** (`"1.0.0.0"` -> `"1.0.0"`), not an instance method.
- **Extraction/parse helpers are on `PackageExtractor` (static)**, not on `NuGetClient`.
- **`GetVersionsAsync` is already in ascending NuGet version order** — oldest is `list[0]`,
  newest is `list[^1]`. Do NOT re-sort the list yourself: a naive string/`OrderBy` sort is
  wrong (`"10.0.1"` sorts before `"3.5.8"` lexically). Trust the returned order; use
  `GetLatestVersionAsync` for "latest stable".
- **Async, Task-returning, `*Async` suffix** on every network call; nullable returns where a
  package/version may not exist (`GetLatestVersionAsync`, `ResolveVersionPatternAsync`).

## After it builds: show your work

Show the final program and name the NuGetFetch calls used (e.g. `GetLatestVersionAsync`,
`PackageExtractor.IsValidPackage`). This library shadows the official NuGet client, so
surfacing the calls proves you used NuGetFetch — not a hallucinated `NuGet.Protocol` shape.

# NuGetFetch — download/extract/query NuGet packages from .NET

A small NuGet client. Package `NuGetFetch`, namespace `NuGetFetch`. AOT-compatible
(System.Text.Json source-gen, no reflection). Do NOT reach for the official
`NuGet.Protocol`/`NuGet.Client` types (`SourceRepository`, `FindPackageByIdResource`,
`PackageMetadataResource`, …) — none of those exist here.

## Core pattern (bring your own HttpClient)

```csharp
using NuGetFetch;

HttpClient http = new();             // YOU own it. NuGetClient has no parameterless ctor.
NuGetClient client = new(http);      // pass the HttpClient in.

string? latest = await client.GetLatestVersionAsync("Newtonsoft.Json");          // "13.0.4" or null
IReadOnlyList<string> all = await client.GetVersionsAsync("Newtonsoft.Json");    // ascending; all[0] is oldest
string? v = await client.ResolveVersionPatternAsync("Newtonsoft.Json", "12.0.*"); // "12.0.3"

await client.DownloadToFileAsync("Newtonsoft.Json", "13.0.3", "/tmp/n.nupkg");   // write .nupkg to disk
using Stream s = await client.DownloadAsync("Newtonsoft.Json", "13.0.3");        // or get a stream
```

## Extraction is a SEPARATE static class (not a NuGetClient method)

```csharp
string dir = PackageExtractor.Extract("/tmp/n.nupkg", "/tmp/out");        // from a file
string dir2 = await PackageExtractor.ExtractAsync(stream, "/tmp/out");    // from a DownloadAsync stream
bool ok = PackageExtractor.IsValidPackage(dir);
bool hasDlls = PackageExtractor.HasManagedLibraries(dir);
PackageIdentity? id = PackageExtractor.ParsePackageReference("Newtonsoft.Json@13.0.1"); // .Id, .Version
```

## Search is a SEPARATE class

```csharp
SearchService search = new(http);
IReadOnlyList<SearchResult> r = await search.SearchByPrefixAsync("Newtonsoft", take: 5); // r[i].Id
IReadOnlyList<SearchResult> q = await search.SearchAsync("json", take: 20, prerelease: false);
```

## Gotchas (where intuition is wrong)

- **No internal HttpClient.** `new NuGetClient()` does not exist; always `new NuGetClient(http)`.
  Same for `SearchService(http)`. Reuse one `HttpClient`.
- **`NuGetClient.NormalizeVersion(v)` is STATIC** (`"1.0.0.0"` -> `"1.0.0"`), not an instance method.
- **Extraction/parse helpers are on `PackageExtractor` (static)**, not on `NuGetClient`.
- **`GetVersionsAsync` is already in ascending NuGet version order** — oldest is `list[0]`,
  newest is `list[^1]`. Do NOT re-sort the list yourself: a naive string/`OrderBy` sort is
  wrong (`"10.0.1"` sorts before `"3.5.8"` lexically). Trust the returned order; use
  `GetLatestVersionAsync` for "latest stable".
- **Async, Task-returning, `*Async` suffix** on every network call; nullable returns where a
  package/version may not exist (`GetLatestVersionAsync`, `ResolveVersionPatternAsync`).

Contents of https://github.com/richlander/dotnet-inspect:
CLI tool for inspecting .NET libraries and NuGet packages. It is for .NET what `docker inspect` and `kubectl describe` are for container land: view package metadata, API surfaces, dependencies, source provenance, and version-to-version changes.

## Install or run

[](#install-or-run)

dotnet tool install -g dotnet-inspect
dotnet-inspect <command\>

Run without installing:

dnx dotnet-inspect -y -- <command\>

## What it inspects

[](#what-it-inspects)

Source

Examples

Notes

NuGet packages

`package System.Text.Json`, `type --package Markout`

Supports versions, custom sources, `nuget.config`, TFMs, package layout, dependencies, and vulnerabilities.

Restored projects

`project ./src/App --agents-index`, `project ./src/App --readme Markout`

Resolves direct package references from `project.assets.json` for compact package grounding manifests and version-pinned docs.

Platform libraries

`library System.Private.CoreLib`, `library System.Text.Json --version 10.0.0`, `diff --platform System.Runtime@9.0.0..10.0.0`

Resolves installed SDK/runtime assemblies, including runtime-only implementation assemblies with no NuGet package.

Local assets

`library ./bin/MyLib.dll`, `package ./pkg/MyLib.nupkg`

Useful for auditing builds before publishing.

Bare names are routed automatically: platform-looking names (`System.*`, `Microsoft.AspNetCore.*`) resolve to installed platform libraries; other names resolve as NuGet packages. In API commands, common CoreLib aliases and simple type names such as `string`, `int`, `DateTime`, and `Guid` resolve to `System.Private.CoreLib`. Use explicit commands and `--package`, `--platform`, or `--library` when you need a specific source.

Type search, member tables, docs, overload selection, generics, obsolete-member markers, direct calls and callers, source/decompiled/IL drill-in.

# NuGetFetch — download/extract/query NuGet packages from .NET

A small NuGet client. Package `NuGetFetch`, namespace `NuGetFetch`. AOT-compatible
(System.Text.Json source-gen, no reflection). Do NOT reach for the official
`NuGet.Protocol`/`NuGet.Client` types (`SourceRepository`, `FindPackageByIdResource`,
`PackageMetadataResource`, …) — none of those exist here.

## Core pattern (bring your own HttpClient)

```csharp
using NuGetFetch;

HttpClient http = new();             // YOU own it. NuGetClient has no parameterless ctor.
NuGetClient client = new(http);      // pass the HttpClient in.

string? latest = await client.GetLatestVersionAsync("Newtonsoft.Json");          // "13.0.4" or null
IReadOnlyList<string> all = await client.GetVersionsAsync("Newtonsoft.Json");    // ascending; all[0] is oldest
string? v = await client.ResolveVersionPatternAsync("Newtonsoft.Json", "12.0.*"); // "12.0.3"

await client.DownloadToFileAsync("Newtonsoft.Json", "13.0.3", "/tmp/n.nupkg");   // write .nupkg to disk
using Stream s = await client.DownloadAsync("Newtonsoft.Json", "13.0.3");        // or get a stream
```

## Extraction is a SEPARATE static class (not a NuGetClient method)

```csharp
string dir = PackageExtractor.Extract("/tmp/n.nupkg", "/tmp/out");        // from a file
string dir2 = await PackageExtractor.ExtractAsync(stream, "/tmp/out");    // from a DownloadAsync stream
bool ok = PackageExtractor.IsValidPackage(dir);
bool hasDlls = PackageExtractor.HasManagedLibraries(dir);
PackageIdentity? id = PackageExtractor.ParsePackageReference("Newtonsoft.Json@13.0.1"); // .Id, .Version
```

## Search is a SEPARATE class

```csharp
SearchService search = new(http);
IReadOnlyList<SearchResult> r = await search.SearchByPrefixAsync("Newtonsoft", take: 5); // r[i].Id
IReadOnlyList<SearchResult> q = await search.SearchAsync("json", take: 20, prerelease: false);
```

## Gotchas (where intuition is wrong)

- **No internal HttpClient.** `new NuGetClient()` does not exist; always `new NuGetClient(http)`.
  Same for `SearchService(http)`. Reuse one `HttpClient`.
- **`NuGetClient.NormalizeVersion(v)` is STATIC** (`"1.0.0.0"` -> `"1.0.0"`), not an instance method.
- **Extraction/parse helpers are on `PackageExtractor` (static)**, not on `NuGetClient`.
- **`GetVersionsAsync` is already in ascending NuGet version order** — oldest is `list[0]`,
  newest is `list[^1]`. Do NOT re-sort the list yourself: a naive string/`OrderBy` sort is
  wrong (`"10.0.1"` sorts before `"3.5.8"` lexically). Trust the returned order; use
  `GetLatestVersionAsync` for "latest stable".
- **Async, Task-returning, `*Async` suffix** on every network call; nullable returns where a
  package/version may not exist (`GetLatestVersionAsync`, `ResolveVersionPatternAsync`).

## After it builds: show your work

Show the final program and name the NuGetFetch calls used (e.g. `GetLatestVersionAsync`,
`PackageExtractor.IsValidPackage`). This library shadows the official NuGet client, so
surfacing the calls proves you used NuGetFetch — not a hallucinated `NuGet.Protocol` shape.

</skill-context>

<!-- GENERATED from AGENTS.md by eng/sync-skill.sh. Do not edit. -->

# NuGetFetch — download/extract/query NuGet packages from .NET

A small NuGet client. Package `NuGetFetch`, namespace `NuGetFetch`. AOT-compatible
(System.Text.Json source-gen, no reflection). Do NOT reach for the official
`NuGet.Protocol`/`NuGet.Client` types (`SourceRepository`, `FindPackageByIdResource`,
`PackageMetadataResource`, …) — none of those exist here.

## Core pattern (bring your own HttpClient)

```csharp
using NuGetFetch;

HttpClient http = new();             // YOU own it. NuGetClient has no parameterless ctor.
NuGetClient client = new(http);      // pass the HttpClient in.

string? latest = await client.GetLatestVersionAsync("Newtonsoft.Json");          // "13.0.4" or null
IReadOnlyList<string> all = await client.GetVersionsAsync("Newtonsoft.Json");    // ascending; all[0] is oldest
string? v = await client.ResolveVersionPatternAsync("Newtonsoft.Json", "12.0.*"); // "12.0.3"

await client.DownloadToFileAsync("Newtonsoft.Json", "13.0.3", "/tmp/n.nupkg");   // write .nupkg to disk
using Stream s = await client.DownloadAsync("Newtonsoft.Json", "13.0.3");        // or get a stream
```

## Extraction is a SEPARATE static class (not a NuGetClient method)

```csharp
string dir = PackageExtractor.Extract("/tmp/n.nupkg", "/tmp/out");        // from a file
string dir2 = await PackageExtractor.ExtractAsync(stream, "/tmp/out");    // from a DownloadAsync stream
```

crossScenarioIssues: Every prompt uses the phrase 'Using THAT library' to explicitly instruct the agent to use NuGetFetch. This creates systematic bias favoring the skilled agent over a baseline, which would waste time searching for or inferring the library name. A neutral prompt would describe the task without naming or referencing the library.

All 12 file_contains assertions (GetLatestVersionAsync, GetVersionsAsync, DownloadToFileAsync, PackageExtractor×2, SearchByPrefixAsync, ResolveVersionPatternAsync) are narrow gates that test vocabulary/method names rather than outcomes. This pattern assumes the only valid solution uses these exact APIs from NuGetFetch.

Output format is rigidly specified across all scenarios (exact prefixes like 'latest:', 'oldest:', 'downloaded:', 'valid:', 'pkg:', 'resolved:', 'normalized:' and exact regex patterns). An agent producing equivalent information in a different format (e.g., 'Version: 13.0.4' instead of 'latest: 13.0.4') would fail despite being correct.

Anchor values are deterministic but highly specific (3.5.8, 13.0.3, 12.0.3, 1.0.0, Newtonsoft.Json.Bson). These lock answers to live nuget.org state rather than testing the logic that would produce them.

Six of 18 rubric items are classified as 'technique' (prescribing HOW rather than testing WHAT), compared to only 6 'outcome' items. This skews toward testing implementation details and skill-specific patterns rather than correct results.
overfittingResult.overallReasoning: This evaluation shows moderate-to-high overfitting through three compounding mechanisms: (1) all 6 prompts use 'Using THAT library' to explicitly instruct skilled agents to use NuGetFetch while baseline agents cannot follow this directive, creating systematic bias; (2) all 12 assertions are narrow gates testing for specific API method names (GetLatestVersionAsync, PackageExtractor, etc.) rather than testing outcomes, which locks success to NuGetFetch usage rather than correctness; (3) output format is rigidly specified with exact prefixes and regexes, so agents producing correct answers in alternative formats fail. However, the overfitting is partially justified by the fact that NuGetFetch is genuinely unknown in model training data and the skill teaches unique architectural patterns with no practical alternative when prompts forbid hand-rolled and official APIs. The core reason for moderate rather than high overfitting: the skill does teach something genuinely novel, but the eval conflates 'uses NuGetFetch' (narrow gate) with 'produces correct result' (outcome).
